ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sweet and sour pork; mapped to the broader 'pork dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a deep-fried pork dish with sweet-sour sauce; no specific composite dish code exists in FoodEx2 for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a dedicated code for sweet and sour pork as a composite dish. A0CKP (cooked pork) is used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for sweet and sour pork as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sweet and sour pork in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising several pieces of battered deep-fried pork tenderloin coated in sweet-sour sauce with bell peppers and pineapple.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet and Sour Pork (糖醋里脊) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sweet and Sour Pork (糖醋里脊), cr_id G00010140, with confidence 0.95. The HPB record reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g and sugar as 0.0 g/100g; this is noted as a known data limitation — the HPB entry likely reflects the meat component only and does not capture the full dish including sweet-sour sauce and batter. This limitation is documented in notes. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB source; set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Chinese Dishes — Analogous estimate based on deep-fried battered pork with sweet-sour sauce; reference: University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweet and sour pork / battered fried foods with sugar-based sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB source records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g for this entry, which is a known underestimate (the HPB record likely reflects the pork component only). The full dish — including cornstarch batter and sweet-sour sauce — would carry a meaningful carbohydrate load. GI of 55 is estimated based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sweet and sour pork (battered, deep-fried, with sugar-vinegar sauce), which is consistent with the mixed carbohydrate profile of cornstarch batter (high GI ~95) and sugar-based sauce diluted by the protein-dominant pork component. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100; because the HPB-reported carbohydrate for the pork component is 0.0 g, the GL is recorded as 0.0 against the HPB nutrient basis, with the caveat that the true GL of the full dish is materially higher. Clinicians should treat this dish as moderate-to-high GL in practice. A GI of 55 is considered low on the GI scale (55 or less).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; derived from soy sauce and ketchup in the sweet-sour sauce
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit sweet and sour pork; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is 175% of the 600 mg/100g upper limit, and the low potassium content (47 mg/100g) provides negligible offsetting effect — request a reduced-sodium preparation with less soy sauce and ketchup, or substitute with a lower-sodium sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried cornstarch batter and sugar-rich sweet-sour sauce substantially raise glycaemic load despite the HPB record showing 0 g carbohydrate (pork component only)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this dish as high glycaemic load in practice; the cornstarch batter coating and sweet-sour sauce typically contribute 15–25 g sugar and significant refined starch per 100 g of full dish — request a reduced-sugar sauce, avoid the batter coating, and limit portion size to minimise postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying in oil raises saturated and total fat intake; high sodium (1050 mg/100g) contributes to arterial stiffness; cholesterol at 89 mg/100g adds to LDL burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of sweet and sour pork due to the combination of deep-frying (elevated saturated fat and total fat), high sodium (1050 mg/100g), and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) — all of which collectively increase cardiovascular risk; a steamed or pan-seared preparation with reduced-sodium sauce is a safer alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); pork contains arachidonic acid; refined sugar in sauce is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sweet and sour pork; deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation, pork is a high-arachidonic-acid red meat, and the sweet-sour sauce adds refined sugar — all three factors are pro-inflammatory for RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet and Sour Pork (糖醋里脊, Táng Cù Lǐ Jǐ) is a classic Chinese dish consisting of pork tenderloin pieces coated in a cornstarch-based batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a vibrant sweet-sour sauce made from ketchup, rice vinegar, sugar, and soy sauce, typically garnished with bell peppers, pineapple chunks, and onion. The dish originates from Chinese culinary tradition and is widely popular in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The texture contrast — crispy battered exterior against tender, juicy pork — is the defining characteristic. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, and 10.5 g fat per 100 g, with sodium at a clinically significant 1050 mg/100 g. The HPB entry records 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar, which is a known limitation reflecting the pork component only; the full dish including batter and sauce carries a substantially higher carbohydrate and sugar load. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension (sodium 1050 mg/100g), Type 2 Diabetes (high glycaemic load from batter and sugar sauce), and cardiovascular disease (deep-fried fat, high sodium, cholesterol 89 mg/100g). It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to AGE generation from deep-frying and pro-inflammatory ingredients. A reduced-sodium, reduced-sugar, lighter-preparation version is recommended for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010140, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The match is exact. The known limitation of 0 g carbohydrate/sugar in the HPB record is documented and does not reduce source confidence — it reflects the scope of the HPB entry rather than a data error.
notes: ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is recorded as 0.0 and sugar_g is recorded as 0.0 per the HPB Singapore Foodo source (cr_id G00010140). This is NOT naturally zero — the HPB entry is understood to reflect the pork component of the dish only and does not capture the full dish carbohydrate contribution from the cornstarch batter and sweet-sour sauce. This is a known data limitation of the HPB source for this record. The zero values are preserved as reported by the approved source; clinicians and users should treat the full dish as carrying a materially higher carbohydrate and sugar load (estimated 15–25 g sugar per 100 g of sauce component). dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 per HPB source; this is plausible for the pork-dominant component recorded. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB sweet and sour pork entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. GI NOTE: The HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g means the calculated glycemic load per serving against the HPB nutrient basis is 0.0; however, the true GL of the full dish is materially higher and clinicians should treat this as a moderate-to-high GL dish in practice. GI of 55 is considered low on the GI scale (55 or less) and is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered deep-fried pork with sweet-sour sauce. HALAL STATUS: Sweet and sour pork contains pork and is not halal.
